text.title: MVP Scoping Framework
text.summary: Six-step process to scope an MVP: define the problem, identify core value, list features, apply MoSCoW, validate scope, and define done — with a hard capacity constraint on Must-Have features.

section.title: What is an MVP
section.p: Minimum Viable Product is the smallest version that solves the core problem, delivers value to early adopters, and allows you to learn and iterate. The critical distinction: MVP is about learning, not about launching a polished product.

rule.statement: Every MVP must state a falsifiable hypothesis and a numeric kill criterion before the feature list is written.
rule.rationale: Without a hypothesis, scope decisions have no anchor. Without a kill criterion, the MVP becomes a permanent v1 even when metrics miss.

rule.statement: Ship an MVP to validate a hypothesis; evolve to MLP (Minimum Lovable Product) only after validation.
rule.rationale: MVP targets early adopters who tolerate rough edges; MLP targets retention-sensitive mainstream users. Conflating the two inflates scope before the hypothesis is proven.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Define the core problem using the template: "For [user] who [problem], our MVP proves [hypothesis] by delivering [outcome] in [timebox] with [constraints]."
rule.rationale: Forces the problem statement to name a specific user, a hypothesis, and a timebox before any features appear.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Identify the ONE thing the product must do well; apply the value test: if you remove it, is the product useless?
rule.rationale: Single core value prevents multi-axis scope — the most common cause of a 6-month MVP.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Brain-dump all features, then categorize each as Core, Important, Nice-to-have, or Out of Scope.
rule.rationale: Separation forces explicit trade-offs before MoSCoW weighting.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Apply MoSCoW: Must Have ≤ 60% of capacity, Should Have ≤ 20%, reserve 20% buffer. Won't Have must be explicit and list ≥ 3 items.
rule.rationale: The capacity constraint is a physical ceiling, not a guideline. Won't Have is the scope-creep firewall.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Validate scope against five gates: solves core problem end-to-end; first user can complete core job; value is delivered without future features; buildable in target timebox; hypothesis is testable.
rule.rationale: Each gate catches a different failure mode — incompleteness, user-flow gaps, dependency holes, schedule risk, and unverifiability.

rule.statement: Step 6 — Define done: acceptance criteria per Must-Have feature, definition of "good enough" quality, metrics to collect, and the next decision after MVP.
rule.rationale: Without a definition of done, the MVP ship date is always "almost ready."

text.reference: Full MVP scope document with all sections
Capacity guard script: fails if Must days exceed 60% budget
